candidate_problems.text: Is the apparent fine-tuning of complexity genuinely a systems-level lock-in in the space of possible laws, or mainly an artifact of our current parameterization that varies constants independently instead of deriving them from deeper coupled structure? This is the central unresolved issue because the conjecture's force depends on whether coordinated families of constants exist that preserve complexity.

How should 'complexity-permitting' be defined and measured across possible universes in a non-anthropocentric, theory-neutral way? Without a principled metric for complexity, chemical richness, long-lived gradients, and open-ended structure formation, the conjecture risks being impressionistic and cannot distinguish narrow basins from broad viable regions.

What is the topology of viability in possibility space: are there multiple disconnected basins of mutually stabilizing physics that support complex structure, or only one tiny island near our constants? This matters because the equilibrium-selection analogy changes substantially depending on whether coordinated moves can reach alternative complexity-supporting regimes.
